perturbation.original_text: Any payments due under this Agreement shall be due on such date as specified in this Agreement and, in the event such date is not a Business Day, then the next succeeding Business Day. In the event that any payment due under this Agreement is not made when due, the amount due shall accrue interest beginning on the [***] following the date on which such payment was due, calculated at the [***] for the due date, or, if lower, the maximum rate permitted by law, calculated from the due date until paid in full.
perturbation.changed_text: Any payments due under this Agreement shall be due on such date as specified in this Agreement and, in the event such date is not a Business Day, then the next succeeding Business Day. In the event that any payment due under this Agreement is not made when due, the amount due shall accrue interest beginning on the 91st day following the date on which such payment was due, calculated at the [***] for the due date, or, if lower, the maximum rate permitted by law, calculated from the due date until paid in full.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the start date for accruing interest on late payments to the 91st day contradicts state laws on timely payments and interest accrual.
perturbation.law_explanation: Massachusetts law requires payment of wages, including fringe benefits and other compensation, within a specific timeframe. By delaying the accrual of interest on late payments to 91 days, the modified text contradicts the spirit and potentially the letter of M.G.L. c. 149, § 148, which aims to ensure timely compensation for work performed. Payments beyond this 90 day period may be considered a wage violation.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: General Law - Part I, Title XXI, Chapter 149, Section 148 Skip to Content General Laws Part I ADMINISTRATION OF THE GOVERNMENT Title XXI LABOR AND INDUSTRIES Chapter 149 LABOR AND INDUSTRIES Section 148 Payment of wages; commissions; exemption by contract; persons deemed employers; provision for cashing check or draft; violation of statute Section 148 Search Search the Legislature Search General Laws Go Directly to a General Law Chapter Section Go to General Law Skip to Content Print Page Prev ious Next Section 148: Payment of wages; commissions; exemption by contract; persons deemed employers; provision for cashing check or draft; violation of statute Section 148. Every person having employees in his service shall pay weekly or bi-weekly each such employee the wages earned by him to within six days of the termination of the pay period during which the wages were earned if employed for five or six days in a calendar week, or to within seven days of the termination of the pay period during which the wages were earned if such employee is employed seven days in a calendar week, or in the case of an employee who has worked for a period of less than five days, hereinafter called a casual employee, shall, within seven days after the termination of such period, pay the wages earned by such casual employee during such period, but any employee leaving his employment shall be paid in full on the following regular pay day, and, in the absence of a regular pay day, on the following Saturday; and any employee discharged from such employment shall be paid in full on the day of his discharge, or in Boston as soon as the laws requiring pay rolls, bills and accounts to be certified shall have been complied with; and the commonwealth, its departments, officers, boards and commissions shall so pay every mechanic, workman and laborer employed by it or them, and every person employed in any other capacity by it or them in any penal or charitable institution, and every county and city shall so pay every employee engaged in its business the wag

perturbation.justification: The changed text delays the accrual of interest on late payments to 91 days, which contradicts the general spirit of timely wage payment laws. While the snippets directly address wage payments (M.G.L. c. 149, § 148) and stipulate payment within a short timeframe (6-7 days), the changed contract introduces a significant delay (91 days) before interest accrues on overdue amounts. This delay undermines the prompt payment of dues, conflicting with the broader aim of ensuring timely compensation, as explained in the law explanation, and therefore creates a legal contradiction related to timely payment.

perturbation.original_text: At least twenty-four (24) months from the end of the Initial Term, Vericel shall provide MediWound notice whether Vericel elects to extend the Initial Term of the Agreement by an additional twenty four (24) months. After the Initial Term (including any extension thereto made in accordance with the preceding sentence), the Agreement may be extended on a yearly basis up to ten (10) years at Vericel's sole discretion, with renewal notice to be provided to MediWound no later than twelve (12) months prior to the expiry of any yearly extension (the "Renewal Term", and the Initial Term, together with the Renewal Term, if any, the "Term"); provided that unless otherwise agreed by the Parties, the Term of this Agreement (including the Initial Term, any extension of the Initial Term and any Renewal Terms) shall be no more than fifteen (15) years in total.
perturbation.changed_text: At least one (1) month from the end of the Initial Term, Vericel shall provide MediWound notice whether Vericel elects to extend the Initial Term of the Agreement by an additional twenty four (24) months. After the Initial Term (including any extension thereto made in accordance with the preceding sentence), the Agreement may be extended on a yearly basis up to ten (10) years at Vericel's sole discretion, with renewal notice to be provided to MediWound no later than one (1) month prior to the expiry of any yearly extension (the "Renewal Term", and the Initial Term, together with the Renewal Term, if any, the "Term"); provided that unless otherwise agreed by the Parties, the Term of this Agreement (including the Initial Term, any extension of the Initial Term and any Renewal Terms) shall be no more than fifteen (15) years in total.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the notice period for renewal to one month may be insufficient for MediWound to adequately prepare, potentially violating the implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing under New York law, which governs the agreement.
perturbation.law_explanation: New York law, through NY UCC § 1-304, imposes an obligation of good faith in the performance and enforcement of contracts. Reducing the notification period to a single month could be seen as a failure to act in good faith, as it may not provide the supplier (MediWound) with sufficient time to make necessary adjustments to their business operations in preparation for the contract's potential non-renewal. This modification could be deemed to frustrate the purpose of the contract, which is to ensure a stable supply of product.

Obligation of Good Faith. Every contract or duty within this act imposes an obligation of good faith in its performance and enforcement.
perturbation.justification: The changed text reduces the notification period from twenty-four months to one month. The law explanation states that this reduction could be seen as a failure to act in good faith under NY UCC § 1-304, as it may not provide sufficient time for necessary adjustments. Scraped Law Snippet 1, referencing NY UCC § 1-304, explicitly states, 'Every contract or duty within this act imposes an obligation of good faith in its performance and enforcement.' Reducing the notification period to one month contradicts this obligation because it potentially hinders MediWound's ability to prepare for the contract's potential non-renewal, violating the principle of good faith. This aligns with the 'Inconsistencies Legal Contradiction' definition, where a time-sensitive obligation does not align with legal requirements, making the contractual terms unenforceable or illegal.

perturbation.original_text: In the event either Party learns that it, any of its employees or contractors, any Supplier or any of a Supplier's employees or contractors has been debarred, it shall notify the other Party promptly, and in any event within [***] of learning of such debarment. In the event that MediWound, any of its employees or contractors, any Supplier or any of a Supplier's employees or contractors has been debarred, MediWound shall immediately remove or have removed such Person from thereafter performing Manufacturing or supply activities under this Agreement with respect to the Product upon learning of such debarment. In the event that Vericel, any of its employees or contractors, any Sublicensee or any of such Sublicensee's employees or contractors has been debarred, it shall immediately remove or have removed such Person from thereafter performing distribution activities under this Agreement with respect to the Product upon learning of such debarment.
perturbation.changed_text: In the event either Party learns that it, any of its employees or contractors, any Supplier or any of a Supplier's employees or contractors has been debarred, it shall notify the other Party promptly, and in any event within thirty (30) days of learning of such debarment. In the event that MediWound, any of its employees or contractors, any Supplier or any of a Supplier's employees or contractors has been debarred, MediWound shall immediately remove or have removed such Person from thereafter performing Manufacturing or supply activities under this Agreement with respect to the Product upon learning of such debarment. In the event that Vericel, any of its employees or contractors, any Sublicensee or any of such Sublicensee's employees or contractors has been debarred, it shall remove or have removed such Person from thereafter performing distribution activities under this Agreement with respect to the Product 180 days after learning of such debarment.
perturbation.explanation: Providing a 180-day delay for removing debarred individuals from distribution activities contradicts federal regulations requiring immediate removal to protect the integrity of pharmaceutical supply chains.
perturbation.law_explanation: 21 U.S.C. § 335a grants the FDA authority to debar individuals and companies from participating in the pharmaceutical industry. By allowing a 180-day delay in removing a debarred individual from distribution activities, the modified clause directly conflicts with the intent of debarment, which is to immediately prevent untrustworthy parties from involvement in the supply chain. This delay creates a window during which potentially compromised products could be distributed, undermining the public health protections afforded by the law.

Debarment, temporary denial of approval, and suspension (a) Mandatory debarment; certain drug applications (1) Corporations, partnerships, and associations If the Secretary finds that a person other than an individual has been convicted, after May 13, 1992, of a felony under Federal law for conduct relating to the deve
perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces a 180-day delay for removing debarred individuals from distribution activities. The Law Explanation states that this conflicts with the intent of debarment, which is to immediately prevent untrustworthy parties from involvement in the supply chain. The Perturbation Explanation also notes that this delay contradicts federal regulations requiring immediate removal. Scraped Law Snippet 1 mentions 21 USC 335a, relating to 'Debarment, temporary denial of approval, and suspension,' implying immediate action is necessary upon debarment, which the 180-day delay undermines.
